coredata.dc:title: Construction of a molecularly imprinted electroluminescence sensor for pyrimethanil detection via dummy template imprinting combined with click-chemical labeling of mimic enzymes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Lijuan
Gao, Peng
Wu, Minjin
Luo, Kui
Li, Jianping
coredata.dc:description:
Background Molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) sensors have been used to detect trace amounts of pesticide residues. But the difficulty of inert analytes in producing measurable signals often limits the application of MIP sensors. The inherent limitations of the signal transduction mechanism impose fundamental constraints on the enhancement of detection sensitivity.
Results Herein, a strategy was proposed for sensitive, selective and rapid determination of trace pyrimethanil (PMT) residues using MIP sensors by the aid of dummy template imprinting. MIP film was synthesized using vinyl pyrimethanil (Abbr. PMT-Vi), a structural analogue of PMT, as the dummy template molecule. After the formation of MIP film, analyte PMT was added for competing with PMT-Vi in the imprinted cavities of MIP. Then, Fe/N-PAMAM-SH, a dendritic polymer bound with Fe/N CDs, was immobilized onto the MIP via the Thiol-ene click reaction between Fe/N-PAMAM-SH and PMT-Vi. PMT was indirectly determined by measuring the ECL signal of Fe/N CDs in the luminol-hydrogen peroxide system.
Significance The strategy greatly enhances the detection sensitivity of MIP sensors, achieving a detection limit of 2.58 × 10−16 mol/L. It also offers a novel approach for detecting molecules that lack characteristic signals or generate weak signals.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pesticide residue
Molecularly imprinted sensor
Dummy template
Click-chemistry
Mimic enzymes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of Fe/N CDs
Characterization of click reaction products on the surface of MIP films
Imprinting factors and the adsorption kinetics of MIP
Response of the MIP-ECL sensor to PMT in different concentration
Reproducibility and stability of the MIP-ECL sensors

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the support given by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22264010 ) and Guangxi Colleges and Universities Key Laboratory of Food Safety and Detection, China .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the preparing of molecularly imprinted sensor and detect of PMT based on dummy template imprinting combined with click-chemical labeling of mimic enzymes.

(A) UV–Vis spectra of Fe/N-PAMAM-SH in the presence of TMB (a); Fe/N-PAMAM-SH in the presence of H2O2 (b); TMB and H2O2 (c); Fe/N CDs in the presence of TMB and H2O2 (d); Fe/N-PAMAM-SH in the presence of TMB and H2O2 (e). (B) ECL intensity of the MIP electrode in the luminol-H2O2 system before click reaction (a); NIP electrode in the luminol-H2O2 system before click reaction (b); NIP electrode in the luminol-H2O2 system after click reaction without PMT (c); MIP electrode in the luminol-H2O2 system after click reaction without PMT (d).
(A) Infrared spectroscopy of Fe/N-PAMAM-SH; (B) XPS spectra of Fe/N-PAMAM-SH; (C) C1s spectra; (D) N1s spectra; (E) O1s spectra; (F) Fe2p spectra.

(A) CV curves of GCE (a), MIP electrode (b), MIP sensor after elution of PMT-Vi (c), MIP sensor after re-adsorption of PMT-Vi (d), NIP sensor (e), and NIP sensor after elution (f), inset figure is the enlarged CV plots of MIP sensor (b), NIP sensor (e), and NIP sensor after elution (f). (B) EIS spectra of GCE (a), MIP electrode (b), MIP sensor after elution of PMT-Vi (c), MIP sensor after re-adsorption of PMT-Vi (d), inset figure is the enlarged EIS plots of GCE (a).
SEM images of (A) MIP and (B) NIP; AFM image of MIP (C); and AFM height profile of MIP (D).
ECL response curves of the MIP-ECL sensor (A) and the calibration curve (B)

Responses of the MIP-ECL sensor to PMT (a) and 100x imidacloprid (b), trifluralin (c), isoprocarb (d), prochloraz (e), glyphosate (f), bromacil (g), propoxur (h), thiacloprid (i), atrazine (j), omethoate (k), profenofos (l), fenthion (m), parathion-methyl (n), pendimethalin (o), carboxin (p), glucose (q), fructose (r), vitamin C (s), Mn2+ (t), K+ (u), Cr3+ (v), Cu2+ (w), Zn2+ (x), Fe3+ (y), Ni2+ (z), Al3+ (α), and Ca2+ (β).
Comparison of analytical performances of the PMT detection methods.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: QDs-LFICS: quantum dots-based lateral flow immunochromatography strip.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination results of pyrimethanil concentration in samples.

head.ce:title: Construction of a molecularly imprinted electroluminescence sensor for pyrimethanil detection via dummy template imprinting combined with click-chemical labeling of mimic enzymes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Bioengineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin, 541004, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Bioengineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin, 541004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) sensors have been used to detect trace amounts of pesticide residues. But the difficulty of inert analytes in producing measurable signals often limits the application of MIP sensors. The inherent limitations of the signal transduction mechanism impose fundamental constraints on the enhancement of detection sensitivity.

Herein, a strategy was proposed for sensitive, selective and rapid determination of trace pyrimethanil (PMT) residues using MIP sensors by the aid of dummy template imprinting. MIP film was synthesized using vinyl pyrimethanil (Abbr. PMT-Vi), a structural analogue of PMT, as the dummy template molecule. After the formation of MIP film, analyte PMT was added for competing with PMT-Vi in the imprinted cavities of MIP. Then, Fe/N-PAMAM-SH, a dendritic polymer bound with Fe/N CDs, was immobilized onto the MIP via the Thiol-ene click reaction between Fe/N-PAMAM-SH and PMT-Vi. PMT was indirectly determined by measuring the ECL signal of Fe/N CDs in the luminol-hydrogen peroxide system.

The strategy greatly enhances the detection sensitivity of MIP sensors, achieving a detection limit of 2.58 × 10−16 mol/L. It also offers a novel approach for detecting molecules that lack characteristic signals or generate weak signals.

A highly sensitive molecularly imprinted sensor was constructed for detection of pyrimethanil (PMT) residue in food samples. Molecularly imprinted polymer membranes was synthesized using vinyl pyrimethanil (PMT-Vi), a structural analogue of PMT, as the dummy template molecule. PMT-Vi molecules in the imprinted cavities were replaced by PMT, resulting in a competitive adsorption. Then, Fe/N-PAMAM-SH, a dendritic polymer bound with Fe/N CDs, was immobilized onto the MIP by the click reaction of –SH with PMT-Vi. PMT was indirectly determined by measuring the ECL signal of Fe/N CDs in the luminol-hydrogen peroxide system.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food safety issues caused by pesticide residues in food have attracted great attention from all over the world. In order to ensure food safety, maximum residue limit (MRL) for pesticides in food and agricultural products has been established. Pyrimethanil (PMT) is a high-efficiency pesticide for the prevention and control of gray mold and wilt. However, improperly controlled concentrations or inadequate post-processing processes can leave PMT residues in food due to its limited degradation rate, leading to ingestion and increasing the risk of chronic exposure, and long-term contact or exposure to low doses of PMT can cause a series of health problems [1]. It is crucial to study the detection method of trace PMT to prevent its influence on people's health. The commonly used methods for PMT detection are chromatography [2], fluorescence [3], and immunoassay, but they have inherent limitations, such as expensive equipment, high operating costs, high technical requirements for operators, and potential risk of cross-reactivity. Therefore, it is urgent to develop a new method for PMT detection.

Molecular imprinted polymer (MIP) sensors prepared in conjunction with molecular imprinting can specifically recognize and selectively detect target molecules, and further have the advantages of reusability and wide applicability. The MIP sensors have been widely used in the determination of organic compounds and even biological macromolecules [4] in many fields such as environmental monitoring [5,6], biomedicine [7], food safety [8,9], drug testing [10], chemical identification [11], and so on. Especially, it has been increasingly focused on the detection of trace pesticides in fruits, vegetables and other agricultural products In recent years [12]. However, the determination of PMT by molecularly imprinted sensors has not been reported.

The MRL of PMT in food is low. European Commission, Food and Drug Administration of USA, and Chinese Food and Drug Administration have stipulated the MRL of PMT in different foods respectively. For example, the MRLs of PMT in tomatoes are 1.00 mg/kg, 0.50 mg/kg, and 1.00 mg/kg respectively. Due to the limited sensitivity of traditional MIP sensors, it is difficult to be utilized to the determination of trace pesticide residues in agricultural samples, since most organic molecules cannot produce characteristic signals or only produce weak characteristic signals.

To improve the sensitivity of MIP sensors, signals were amplified by labeling enzymes or catalytic probe molecules [13–15]. In response to this issue, our group has recently introduced a method of click-chemical labeling of liposomes embedding glucose oxidase or probes to enhance the signals and improve the sensitivity of the sensors [16,17]. However, the method suffers from a fatal flaw that the click reaction can only be occurred between specific groups, implying that the method can only detect organic molecules containing click-reactive groups, thus the method cannot be used for PMT determination. Fortunately, a method called dummy template molecular imprinting technology has been proposed [18–22], which selects a dummy template molecule with similar chemical structure, geometry, and dimensional size as the target molecule but may differ in functional groups to prepare the imprinted polymers, and uses the competitive substitution between the target molecule and the dummy template molecule when it is complexed with the imprinted cavities for the analyte determination. By employing virtual template molecular imprinting technology, this approach not only facilitates the detection of hard-to-imprint template molecules but also addresses the challenge of limited template availability [23]. However, the method cannot achieve highly sensitive detection because the dummy molecules do not generate strong measurable signals.

Herein, a new strategy was proposed for ultra-trace analysis based on dummy template imprinting combined with click-chemical labeling of mimic enzymes using MIP sensor, which can be used for the determination of organic compounds such as PMT without click-reactive groups. In order to detect PMT with high sensitivity and selectivity, we prepared a MIP-ECL sensor, and proposed to synthesize molecularly imprinted polymers using 4,6-dimethyl-N-(4-vinylphenyl) pyrimidin-2-amine (i.e., Vinyl pyrimethanil, Abbr., PMT-Vi), which has only one vinyl group than PMT, as the dummy template molecule. The principle of the preparing of the MIP-ECL sensor and the detection of PMT is shown in Fig. 1 . The MIP film was formed by electropolymerization, and then the MIP was placed in the solution of analyte PMT for competitive adsorption. Due to the high structural similarity between PMT and PMT-Vi, some PMT-Vi molecules in the imprinted cavities were replaced by PMT, resulting in the so-called competitive adsorption [24,25]. Finally, the MIP was irradiated under an ultraviolet lamp, so that the PMT-Vi on the imprinted film reacted with the added Fe/N-PAMAM-SH to generate Fe/N-PAMAM-PMT. Fe/N CDs are nanomaterials prepared by doping iron (Fe) and nitrogen (N) elements in carbon dots with efficient peroxidase activity [26]. Due to the mimic peroxidase activity of Fe/N CDs, the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide into ∙OH was catalyzed, which in turn enhanced the ECL intensity of luminol [27]. As the concentration of PMT increases, the ECL intensity decreased, so that PMT detection was realized. To our knowledge, no studies have reported detection using MIP sensors that integrate virtual template technology with click reaction labeling.

Luminol, o-phenylenediamine (O-PD), tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris), L-histidine (His), 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), and N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai. Potassium ferricyanide and hydrogen peroxide (30 %) were purchased from Xilong Chemical Company, Shantou, Guangdong. Polyamidoamine dendrimer (PAMAM) with generation 4.5 (G4.5) were purchased from Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai. 4,6-dimethyl-N-(4-vinylphenyl)pyrimidin-2-amine were purchased from Zhengzhou Huiju Chemical Industry Co., Ltd., Henan.

The MPI-E ECL analyzer (Xi'an Ruimai Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd.) was used to record the electrochemiluminescence. The electrochemical measurement was carried out on the CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd.). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was operated on a PGSTAT128 N electrochemical workstation (Metrohm Co., Ltd.). The three-electrode system was used in all the above electrochemical experiments, in which a modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 3 mm diameter) acts as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) electrode as the reference electrode, and a platinum wire electrode as the counter electrode.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet iS10 Spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., Ltd.). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted on Tecnai G2 F30. In this study, the Bruker Dimension Icon Atomic Force Microscope (Bruker Corporation) was employed. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis was performed using an instrument from Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA. The contact angle was characterized using an XG-CAM Contact Angle Meter (Shanghai Xuanyi Chuangxi Industrial Equipment Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). Potential Analyzer (JEM-2100F, JEOL) was used to characterize the potential before and after click reaction. An ultraviolet lamp with 365 nm was used to trigger the click reaction on the MIP film. The ultrapure water (resistivity>18MQ) used in the experiment was obtained from the ultrapure water system (Sichuan Youpu Ultrapure Technology Co., Ltd.). Liquid Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry (LC-MS) was carried out on LCMS-9030 (Shimadzu, Ltd.).

The preparation of Fe/N CDs was carried out according to references [28,29] with slight improvements. 1.00 g of FeSO4∙7H2O and 0.20 g of L-histidine were weighed, 20 mL of water was added and stirred thoroughly until dissolved. The resulting solution was transferred to a reactor and reacted at 180 °C for 8 h. After the reaction, the products were filtered using a filter with a pore size of 0.22 μm and the filtrate was collected for use.

A 2 mL of 2.00 × 10−3 mol/L mercaptopropionic acid was taken, 5 mL of 2.00 × 10−3 mol/L EDC and 1.25 mL of 2.00 × 10−3 mol/L NHS were added and stirred for 1 h. Another 2 mL of Fe/N CDs were taken, and 5 mL of 2.00 × 10−3 mol/L EDC and 1.25 mL of 2.00 × 10−3 mol/L NHS were added, and stirred for 1 h. The two solutions were mixed, and 400 μL of PAMAM was added, and stirred for 2 h to obtain the Fe/N-PAMAM-SH.

The glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface was polished using 1.0, 0.3 and 0.05 μm alumina powders in turn, and then washed with ultrapure water, ethanol and 1 mol/L sulphuric acid to remove impurities respectively. The MIP primarily utilizes chemical polymerization methods, including in situ polymerization, phase inversion polymerization, photoinitiated polymerization, etc. These methods heavily rely on toxic organic solvents. In order to achieve a more environmentally friendly synthesis process guided by Greenification Principles [30], an electropolymerization method was employed here to prepare the MIP. The structural analogue of pyrimethamine, PMT-Vi (1.00 × 10−3 mol/L), was used as the dummy template molecule, and o-phenylenediamine (4.00 × 10−3 mol/L) was the functional monomer. Electropolymerization was carried out by cyclic voltammetry at 0–1 V at a scan rate of 50 mV/s in a 0.2 mol/L PBS buffer solution (pH = 7.4) for 20 scanning cycles. After the electropolymerization was completed, a mixture of methanol and acetic acid (v/v = 8:3) was used as the elution solvent to remove the dummy template molecules in the MIP to obtain the MIP electrode. The MIP electrode was placed in a solution containing 1.0 × 10−3 mol/L dummy template molecules for readsorption of PMT-Vi. After the readsorption time of 8 min, the MIP electrode was placed in PMT solution for competitive adsorption for 1 h. Then, Fe/N-PAMAM-SH composites were attached to the dummy template molecules by immersing the electrode in a mixed solution of AIBN and Fe/N-PAMAM-SH and performing a click reaction under 365 nm UV light for 70 min.
Non-imprinted film (NIP) was prepared using the same method only without the addition of the dummy template molecule PMT-Vi to the electrolyte solution for electropolymerization.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and alternating impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out on an electrochemical workstation in a 0.005 mol/L K4 [Fe(CN)6]/K3 [Fe(CN)6] solution containing 0.1 mol/L KCl. The CV test was performed in the potential range of −0.2–0.6 V with a scan rate of 50 mV/s. The EIS test was performed with an alternating voltage of 0.005 V and a frequency range of 0.1 kHz–100 kHz.

The prepared MIP electrode was placed in 10 mL 0.05 mol/L Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 8.4) containing 100 μL of 10 μmol/L luminol and 50 μL of 6.45 mmol/L hydrogen peroxide solution for ECL measurement. The test parameters were set as the scanning range of −0.2 to 0.6 V, the scanning speed 0.1 V/s, the photomultiplier tube voltage 900 V, and the sampling rate 10 T/s.

The samples were pre-treated using the method described in Ref. [31]. Tomato, cucumber and apple were mashed separately, 2.00 g of each sample was taken, 3.00 g of anhydrous sodium sulfate was added, and then 10 mL of ethanol was added, and the samples were shaken for 2 min at 150 times/min on a shaker, sonicated for 10 min at 50 kHz on an ultrasonicator with 150 W, and centrifuged for 10 min at 4000 r/min; the supernatant was taken, and the residue was extracted with 10 mL of ethanol for a second time, and the two supernatants were combined in a 20 mL volumetric flask with ethanol.

Fe/N CDs were heavily loaded on the dendrimer PAMAM, which greatly increased the amount of quantum dots. According to Refs. [28,29], it is known that Fe/N CDs possess peroxidase activity and can promote the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide to produce hydroxyl radicals (∙OH), and ∙OH can lead to the enhancement of the ECL of the luminol-hydrogen peroxide system. In order to verify the peroxidase activity of the complex particle clusters, the catalytic effect of Fe/N-PAMAM-SH on the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and luminol in the presence of hydrogen peroxide was studied using UV–Vis spectrophotometry. TMB is one of the commonly used chromogenic substrates for peroxidase, which can be oxidized by hydrogen peroxide in the presence of peroxidase to produce a blue substance. It can be seen from curves a−c in Fig. 2 A that no blue substance was produced when Fe/N-PAMAM-SH, TMB and hydrogen peroxide did not coexist together; however, while these three substances existed at the same time, or Fe/N CDs, TMB and hydrogen peroxide coexisted together, the blue substance was observed, and the absorbance value of the former (curve e) was larger than that of the latter (curve d). This is own to the fact that PAMAM was loaded with a large amount of Fe/N CDs, which has a stronger catalytic effect. In addition, the ECL intensities of the MIP electrodes re-adsorbed with PMT-Vi were examined before and after labeling of Fe/N-PAMAM-SH by click reaction, and the results are shown in Fig. 2B. Before the click reaction, there were no Fe/N-PAMAM-SH on the surface of the MIP, and the ECL signal was weak, which were generated by the oxidation of luminol by hydroxide peroxide. After the click reaction, the ECL increased significantly. It shows that Fe/N-PAMAM-SH can indeed promote the ECL intensity of the luminol-hydrogen peroxide system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of Fe/N CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM image clearly shows that Fe/N CDs are discrete nanoparticles, and the particles are separated from each other without forming agglomerates, and have good dispersion (Fig. S1A). The size distribution of the particles was counted and yielded an average diameter of 6.32 nm (n = 98) (Fig. S1B), which is close to the results in Refs. [28,29].

The surface active groups of Fe/N-PAMAM-SH were investigated using FT-IR, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 A. There is an absorption peak located at 3410 cm−1, which can be attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H/N–H [32]; the typical stretching vibration peak of –SH appeared at 2561 cm−1 [33], and the peak at 1711 cm−1 originates from the CC stretching vibration [34]. In addition, the absorption band observed at 1401 cm−1 is closely related to the vibration of the C–N bond. The band at 1130 cm−1 is typical of Fe–N, and the peak at 620 cm−1 is associated with the deformation vibration of Fe–C, which confirmed the generation of Fe/N CDs [35].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to investigate the chemical elemental composition of Fe/N-PAMAM-SH. From the full-spectrum image (Fig. 3B), it can be confirmed that Fe/N-PAMAM-SH is mainly composed of five elements, namely, C, N, O, Fe, and S. Among them, carbon accounted for 32.63 % of the total, while N, O, Fe, and S accounted for 13.00 %, 44.16 %, 3.43 % and 6.78 %, respectively.

Fig. 3C shows that the fine spectra of C 1s have four characteristic peaks at 284.80, 285.95, and 288.32 eV, respectively, which are attributed to the C–C, C–N, and C–O/CO bonds. It is in agreement with the results of [36]. For the spectra of XPS N 1s (Fig. 3D), three distinct peaks appeared at approximately 399.38 and 401.58 eV, respectively, which correspond to C–N and graphite N groups, respectively, consistent with previous reports [37]. In the O 1s spectra (Fig. 3E), three peaks appear, corresponding to binding energies of 531.51, 532.92 and 529.72 eV, respectively, which are attribute to the CO, C–O and metal-O bands, respectively, in accordance with reference [38]. For the spectra of Fe 2p (Fig. 3F), two characteristic peaks located at about 710.28 and 723.88 eV can be observed, representing Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively, which proves the presence of Fe2+ associated with Fe/N CDs [39]. In addition, we also found two strong peaks at 711.08 and 724.67 eV, which are derived from Fe3+. In summary, the results of XPS further confirm the successful preparation of Fe/N CDs and the transformation of Fe/N CDs into Fe/N-PAMAM-SH after two amidation modifications.

The sensor construction process was characterized by CV and EIS with 0.005 mol/L K4 [Fe(CN)6]/K3 [Fe(CN)6] as a probe in a 10 mL solution containing 0.1 mol/L potassium chloride. As can be seen in Fig. 4 A, the current value of the bare electrode was the largest (curve a), and when MIP was formed on the electrode surface, the current value decreased sharply (curve b), which was attributed to the fact that the molecular cavities on the electrode surface were occupied by dummy template molecules, which blocked the transfer of the probes. As can be seen in Fig. 4A, after the elution step, as the dummy template molecules were eluted off, the formed imprinted cavities acted as ion channels for the probes, resulting in a larger current (curve c). Finally, after 8 min of re-adsorption, the current decreased as the cavities were occupied again (curve d).

The change in resistance during the construction of the MIP electrode was measured by the AC impedance method, and the results are shown in Fig. 4B. The impedance arc of the bare electrode is relatively small as seen in curve a; the impedance arc after polymerization of MIP film increases sharply (curve b), and this change is due to the formation of a dense MIP film on the electrode surface, which leads to a significant increase in the AC impedance value because of the low conductivity of the MIP film. As can be seen in Fig. 4B, When the MIP film was eluted (curve c), imprinted cavities were formed on the MIP film, which acted as channels for the transport of probe ions, and made the impedance value smaller. When the MIP re-adsorbed the dummy template molecules (curve d), the imprinted cavities were reoccupied and the ion channels were blocked, resulting in a larger impedance value again.

Comparing MIP (Fig. 5 A) to NIP (Fig. 5B), MIP appears rougher and shows more bulges on the surface, possibly due to the presence of template molecules in the MIP. The thickness of the MIP film was evaluated by AFM and the results showed that the thickness of the MIP film was approximately 22.75 nm (Fig. 5D).

The FT-IR absorption spectra of MIP and NIP were recorded. NIP was prepared by electrochemical polymerization of o-phenylenediamine, and the N–H stretching vibration peaks appeared at 3441 cm−1, the CN stretching vibration at 1602 cm−1, and the C–N stretching vibration at 1360 cm−1. MIP was obtained by electropolymerization of o-phenylenediamine and template molecules, and it can be seen from Fig. 6 that in addition to the above peaks, new peaks corresponding to template molecules also appeared, such as the peak at 2971 cm−1 corresponds to the methyl group of PMT-Vi.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of click reaction products on the surface of MIP films
ce:section.ce:para: Fe/N-PAMAM-SH was connected with PMT-Vi on MIP by click reaction. In order to verify that the Fe/N-PAMAM-SH had been successfully bound to the MIP surface after the click reaction, the properties of the materials on the MIP surface before and after the click reaction were characterized by EDS energy spectroscopy, Zeta potential and contact angle method, respectively.

An EDS spectrometer was used to characterize the substance on the MIP film before and after the click reaction. The results showed that there was no S element on the MIP (Fig. S2A) before the click reaction, while the peak of S element was found after the click reaction (Fig. S2B), which agrees with that of the Fe/N-PAMAM-SH (Fig. S2C) itself. This result proved that Fe/N-PAMAM-SH had been successfully linked to the dummy template molecule on MIP.

The Zeta potentiometric assay showed that the potential of Fe/N-PAMAM-SH (Fig. S2D curve a) was detected to be about 10 mV and the potential of MIP was about −10 mV (Fig. S2D curve b). After the click reaction, the potential of the MIP film decreased significantly, approaching 0 mV (Fig. S2D curve c). This change indicates that negatively charged Fe/N-PAMAM-SH has been successfully bound to the MIP surface.

The conclusion was further verified by the experimental results of the contact angle. The contact angle of the bare electrode was 63.85 (Fig. S3A), and it decreased to 40.60 after the formation of NIP on the electrode by electropolymerization (Fig. S3B), indicating that the NIP was hydrophilic. The contact angle of the MIP containing the dummy template molecule PMT-Vi was 58.23 (Fig. S3C), indicating that the dummy template molecule PMT-Vi is hydrophobic. The contact angle of the synthesized Fe/N-PAMAM-SH was only 19.45 (Fig. S3D), indicating that the particle has excellent hydrophilicity, which may be related to the large number of hydrophilic groups –NH2 and –SH contained on Fe/N-PAMAM-SH. After the click reaction of Fe/N-PAMAM-SH with the dummy template molecule PMT-Vi, the contact angle decreased from 58.23 in Fig. S3C to 50.81 in Fig. S3E, and the result was in accordance with the expectation.
Therefore, it is proved that Fe/N-PAMAM-SH has been successfully linked to the dummy template molecule PMT-Vi by click reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imprinting factors and the adsorption kinetics of MIP
ce:section.ce:para:
The Imprinting Factor (IF) is an indicator used to evaluate the affinity between molecularly imprinted polymers and template molecules [40]. The IF was calculated by using the ratio of the ECL values recorded after reaching adsorption equilibrium with PMT-Vi between the MIP and the NIP. The adsorption equilibrium times of MIP and NIP were firstly examined, and the results are shown in Fig. 4S
. The concentration of PMT-Vi was fixed 10−8 mol/L, the ECL value of MIP increased with the increase of adsorption time, and reached equilibrium at 10 min with a stabilized value. In contrast, the NIP reached adsorption equilibrium within 15 min. The ECL values were recorded when both reached equilibrium respectively, and the IF was calculated to be 7.75, indicating that MIP has a strong affinity for the template molecule [41].

The adsorption model of MIP on template molecules was further investigated. The adsorption isotherms of MIP were determined in the PMT-Vi concentration range of 1.0 × 10−7 to 5.0 × 10−10 mol/L, and the adsorption isotherms were linearly regressed using Langmuir and Freundlich equations [42]. The results show that the linear correlation coefficients R2 of the Freundlich and Langmuir adsorption isotherms were 0.9810 and 0.9452 (Fig. S5), respectively, and the correlation coefficients of Freundlich are relatively high, which indicates that the adsorption behavior of MIP on PMT-Vi follows the Freundlich adsorption model.

The experimental conditions were optimized, and the results revealed the following key parameters: o-phenylenediamine as the optimal functional monomer (Fig. S6), a template-to-monomer molar ratio of 1:4 (Fig. S7), 20 CV cycles for MIP polymerization (Fig. S8), with competitive and click reaction times of 60 min (Fig. S9) and 70 min (Fig. S10), respectively. In addition, the pH of the electrolyte was 8.4 (Fig. S11), the luminol concentration was 10 μmol/L (Fig. S12), and the hydrogen peroxide concentration was 6.45 mmol/L (Fig. S13).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of the MIP-ECL sensor to PMT in different concentration
ce:section.ce:para: The MIP-ECL sensor was placed in PMT solution for competitive adsorption, and then the click reaction was carried out to connect Fe/N-PAMAM-SH on to MIP. ECL was measured and the results are shown in Fig. 7A. With the increase of PMT concentration, the amount of PMT-Vi that was substituted off the MIP was increasing, the number of connected Fe/N QDs was then decreasing, and the ECL intensity was decreasing. Fig. 7B showed that there was a linear relationship between the reduction in ECL value and the PMT concentration between 1.00 × 10−15 mol/L and 1.00 × 10−9 mol/L, with a linear equation of ΔECL = 1431.7 lgC +711 and a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.994; the limit of detection (LOD, 3σ/S) was calculated to be 2.58 × 10−16 mol/L and the limit of quantification (LOQ, 10σ/S) was calculated to be 8.59 × 10−16 mol/L. Here, σ is the standard deviation of the blank solution response, and S is the slope of the calibration curve.

The analytical performances of the MIP-ECL sensors was compared with that of other common PMT detection methods, as can be seen from Table 1 , the MIP-ECL detection method has a lower detection limit. A MIP electrochemical sensor for PMT detection was fabricated using a similar method, where PMT served as the template molecule. The determination was achieved through differential pulse voltammetry by monitoring the characteristic oxidation current of PMT in pH 6.5 PBS buffer solution (Fig. S14). The sensor demonstrated a LOD of 2.52 × 10−6 mol/L, which was approximately ten orders of magnitude less sensitive than the MIP-ECL sensor presented in this work.

In order to evaluate the specific recognition ability of the MIP-ECL sensor for PMT, the ECL responses of the solution of PMT and the solutions of PMT mixed with 100x interferences respectively were tested. As shown in Fig. 8, these substances had only a very weak effect on the ECL signal of the MIP-ECL sensor for PMT, confirming the sensor's excellent ability to recognize PMT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility and stability of the MIP-ECL sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The reproducibility and stability are parameters that reflect the detection performance of the sensor. The reproducibility of the sensor was examined by measuring the ECL values of the sensor after competition with PMT of 5.00 × 10−12 mol/L, and the results showed that the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the ECL values measured by the same electrode for five determinations was 8.70 %; while the RSD of the ECL values measured by using five different electrodes treated with the same method was 9.53 %. The above results indicated that the intra-batch and inter-batch reproducibility of the determinations were acceptable, respectively. The stability of the sensor was examined by using the sensor for 1.00 × 10−12 mol/L PMT assay. As can be seen from Fig. S15, the ECL value decreases by 5.46 % after 8 days and 8.58 % after three weeks.

Several samples of water, vegetables and fruit were taken. The samples were assayed according to the sample pre-treatment method provided in section 2.7, the concentrations of PMT in samples was quantitatively determined using the conventional calibration curve method. The spike recovery experiment was carried out at the same time. In order to evaluate the accuracy and reliability of the developed sensor, LC-MS (mentioned in Supporting Information) was employed to detect PMT content in the supernatant solutions. The analytical results are shown in Table 2 . The recoveries ranged from 91.75 % to 114.82 % and the RSD of all tests was less than 13 %.

A new method for trace PMT analysis by MIP sensor using dummy template imprinting combined with click-chemical labeling of nanoenzymes was proposed, which greatly enhances the detection sensitivity, but also simplifies the detection procedure. The imprinting factor of MIP was as high as 7.75, indicating that it had high selectivity for PMT. The strategy greatly enhances the detection sensitivity of MIP sensors, achieving a detection limit of 2.58 × 10−16 mol/L. The analytical results of samples show the high accuracy of the sensor. It allows for the convenient detection of organic analytes that do not contain click-reactive groups, and it offers a novel approach for detecting molecules that lack characteristic signals or generate weak signals. The method provides a strategy for the ultra-trace analysis of pesticide residues in food samples.

Lijuan Lin: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Peng Gao: Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation. Minjin Wu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation. Kui Luo: Data curation, Validation. Jianping Li: Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the support given by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22264010) and Guangxi Colleges and Universities Key Laboratory of Food Safety and Detection, China.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhang, X. Zou, Z. Wang, Y. Wang, Y. Zhang, Z. Liu, T. Zhang, Y. Wang, G. Zhang, Y. Tian, Development of a quantum dots -based strip immunoassay for the detection of pyrimethanil in fruit and vegetable samples, J. Food Saf. 41 (2021), https://doi.org/10.1111/jfs.12890.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glutathione modified ag nanoparticles as efficient detector for pyrimethanil
Nanotechnology

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhang, R. Hu, H. Li, Glutathione modified ag nanoparticles as efficient detector for pyrimethanil, Nanotechnology. 30 (2019)115502, https://doi.org/10.1088/1361-6528/aafb97.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multifunctional electrodes based on brochantite/malachite/polyaniline on ni foam for the fabrication of binder-free supercapacitors and pyrimethanil/carbendazim fungicide electrochemical sensors
J. Energy Storage

Energy Storage. 82 (2024)110529, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.est.2024.110529.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of fluquinconazole, pyrimethanil, and clofentezine residues in fruits by liquid chromatography with ultraviolet detection
J. AOAC Int.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Navickiene, M.L. Ribeiro, Determination of fluquinconazole, pyrimethanil, and clofentezine residues in fruits by liquid chromatography with ultraviolet detection, J. Aoac Int. 87 (2004)435-438, https://doi.org/10.1093/jaoac/87.2.435.
